item.title: PRODUCTION
item.description: The recording techniques and technologies that changed what a record could sound like — the studio methods that became standard practice.

item.title: THEORY
item.description: The music-theory concepts the MAP keeps returning to — the harmonic, rhythmic, and formal ideas that recur across otherwise distant genres, artists, and eras.

item.title: SESSION MUSICIANS
item.description: The uncredited players who cut the records — the house bands and hired hands whose work is on the hits without their names on the labels.

item.title: SONGWRITERS
item.description: The writers behind the records — from the staff professionals who supplied other people’s hits to the singer-songwriters who performed their own.

item.title: PRODUCERS
item.description: The figures who shape how a record sounds — the people whose choices about arrangement, room, and tape are audible in the finished track.

item.title: EXECUTIVES
item.description: The people whose decisions determined what got made — the label heads, A&R men, and dealmakers who chose what to fund, sign, and release.

item.title: LABELS
item.description: The record labels with an identity of their own — the houses whose roster, sound, and judgment shaped what their era heard.

item.title: STUDIOS
item.description: The rooms where the records were made — the studios whose acoustics, equipment, and house engineers left an audible signature on a sound.

item.title: ALBUMS
item.description: Full-length records treated as complete artworks — the albums that consolidated a style, defined an artist, or extended what the format could hold.

item.title: SINGLES
item.description: Songs released on their own terms — the 45s and one-off tracks that worked as self-contained statements, on or off an album.
